skill.name: npm-git-install
skill.description: Install npm packages from GitHub repositories using HTTPS or SSH Git URLs, including private repos, pinned branches, tags, and commits.
skill.summary: Guide npm installs from GitHub repositories with safer pinning and authentication practices.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but most are markdown examples in a documentation skill rather than executable code. The confirmed risks are operational: Git-based npm installs can execute package lifecycle scripts, token-based authentication can leak credentials, and global setup commands can alter user systems. No evidence found of prompt injection or malicious hidden behavior.

medium_findings.title: Supply Chain Execution Through Git npm Installs
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install packages directly from GitHub and explains that npm may run prepare scripts during installation. This is legitimate package-management guidance, but untrusted repositories can execute install-time code and should be pinned and reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The markdown context clearly documents npm Git installs and npm lifecycle behavior. The risk is real for untrusted Git repositories, although it is not evidence of malicious intent in the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Guidance Can Expose Tokens
medium_findings.description: The skill shows token-based GitHub installation, a GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable, an .npmrc token entry, and credential helper storage. These are common workflows, but tokens in URLs, shell history, logs, or persistent config can leak repository access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly discusses GitHub tokens and credential storage. It also warns against hardcoding tokens, so the issue is cautionary rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Global Installation and Permission Commands Alter System State
medium_findings.description: The guide includes global npm installs, sudo package installation, sudo ownership changes, shell profile edits, and global Git credential configuration. These commands are expected for setup guidance but can change system security posture if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands are present and can materially affect a user machine. The surrounding text frames them as troubleshooting instructions, so this is an elevated-use risk, not confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many shell-command detections occur inside documentation code fences and command examples. The skill does not include an executable script that automatically runs these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown guide, and the detected commands are inside fenced examples. No evidence found of automatic command execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Symlink Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The symlink command is part of an explanation of npm internals, and the ../ paths are related-skill links. No evidence found that the skill uses these paths to access files outside the workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious paths are visible in markdown context and are not part of executable code. They are best classified as false positives from static pattern matching.

low_findings.title: SSH Private Key Finding Points to Public Key Output
low_findings.description: The SSH-key section reads ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub so the user can register a public key on GitHub. This is not private-key disclosure, although users should still avoid sharing private key material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filename ends in .pub and the surrounding steps describe registering a public SSH key. No evidence found of reading a private key file.

dangerous_patterns.title: npm install git+ URL Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly recommends npm install commands that pull packages from GitHub over HTTPS or SSH. This requires network access and trust in the target repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is central to the skill and is semantically legitimate. It remains dangerous when applied to untrusted repositories because npm can execute package scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo and Global Configuration Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The troubleshooting section includes sudo chown, sudo apt-get, shell profile edits, and global credential helper configuration. These examples can create persistent system changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and their effects are well understood. The guide also discourages indiscriminate sudo, reducing concern about malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Developers often need npm packages from private repositories, forks, or unreleased branches. This skill shows Git URL install patterns with safer pinning, authentication, and troubleshooting guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains npm install syntax for GitHub HTTPS, SSH, branch, tag, and commit references.
Shows how Git URL dependencies can be added to package.json.
Describes private repository installation with SSH keys, GitHub tokens, and .npmrc configuration.
Lists verification commands for global package paths, installed packages, and binaries.
Provides troubleshooting steps for Git installation, npm cache issues, permission errors, and prepare script failures.
limitations: Does not verify the safety of a target GitHub repository.
Does not execute installs or inspect package lifecycle scripts.
Does not manage tokens, SSH keys, or GitHub permissions for the user.
Examples require local npm, Git, network access, and compatible Node.js versions.

use_cases.title: Install a private package
use_cases.description: Use SSH or token-based Git URLs to install an internal Node.js package from GitHub.

use_cases.title: Test an unreleased fix
use_cases.description: Install a package from a specific branch, tag, or commit before it reaches the npm registry.

use_cases.title: Document package setup
use_cases.description: Create repeatable setup instructions for teams that rely on GitHub-hosted npm dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Install From a GitHub Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a public GitHub package.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install an npm package from a GitHub repository branch. Include how to verify the install.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Private Repository
prompt_templates.scenario: Private package install with safer authentication choices.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me install a private GitHub npm package. Compare SSH and token-based options and explain the safer choice.

prompt_templates.title: Pin a Production Dependency
prompt_templates.scenario: Reliable dependency management for production projects.
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to add a GitHub-hosted npm dependency to package.json for production. Recommend a pinned tag or commit approach and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot a Failed Git Install
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced debugging for failed npm installs from Git URLs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose an npm install failure from a GitHub Git URL. Cover Git availability, authentication, prepare scripts, cache, and permissions.

output_examples.input: Install a forked package from a release tag.
output_examples.output: The response recommends using a GitHub dependency pinned to the release tag, then verifying the package and binary after installation.

output_examples.input: Use a private GitHub package without exposing secrets.
output_examples.output: The response explains SSH-based installation first, then describes token-based installation with warnings about shell history, logs, and config files.

output_examples.input: Fix a permission error during global installation.
output_examples.output: The response suggests checking the npm global prefix and using a user-owned npm directory before considering privileged commands.

best_practices: Pin Git dependencies to immutable tags or commits for repeatable installs.
Review package lifecycle scripts before installing from unfamiliar repositories.
Use SSH keys or scoped tokens, and avoid placing tokens directly in dependency URLs.
anti_patterns: Do not depend on a moving main branch for production builds.
Do not paste long-lived tokens into package.json, logs, or shared terminals.
Do not run global sudo or ownership commands without understanding their system impact.

faq.question: Can this skill install packages for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and guidance that a user can review and run in their own environment.

faq.question: Is installing from GitHub as safe as installing from npm?
faq.answer: It can be safe for trusted repositories, but it bypasses normal registry review and may run package scripts during installation.

faq.question: Should I use a branch, tag, or commit?
faq.answer: Use a tag or commit for production. Branches can change and make installs less reproducible.

faq.question: What is the safest private repository method?
faq.answer: SSH keys are usually safer than embedding tokens in URLs, especially when keys are managed and scoped correctly.

faq.question: Why does npm run prepare scripts?
faq.answer: Git dependencies may need a build step before installation. Review those scripts because they execute local commands.

faq.question: Does this skill validate GitHub repositories?
faq.answer: No. Users must inspect repository ownership, source code, package scripts, and dependency history themselves.
